name: Taiwan
tagline: An Austronesian island colonised in turn by Dutch, Spanish, Ming-loyalist, Qing and Japanese hands, and now the seat of a self-governing polity at the centre of an unresolved cross-strait dispute.
summary: Taiwan is a mountainous island off the southeastern coast of the Asian mainland, first home to Austronesian-speaking peoples whose language family is generally traced to the island itself. From the seventeenth century it passed through Dutch and Spanish trading outposts, the Ming-loyalist kingdom founded by Zheng Chenggong (Koxinga), more than two centuries of Qing administration, and fifty years of Japanese colonial rule after the 1895 Treaty of Shimonoseki. Republic of China (ROC) forces took control in 1945, and the ROC central government relocated to the island in December 1949 after its defeat in the Chinese Civil War, since when it has governed Taiwan while the People's Republic of China (PRC), founded that same year, claims the island as a province but has never controlled it. After decades of martial law, an export-led economic miracle and democratisation produced Taiwan's first direct presidential election in 1996; its de jure international status remains contested and unresolved.

founding.label: Relocation of the ROC central government to Taiwan
founding.detail: The polity that governs Taiwan today, the Republic of China, originated on the mainland in 1912; its central government relocated to Taipei in December 1949 after the Nationalists (Kuomintang) were defeated by the Communists in the Chinese Civil War, the same year the People's Republic of China was proclaimed in Beijing. This relocation is the founding moment of the present self-governing polity on the island; it was not a declaration of independence, and the de jure status of Taiwan remains internationally contested and unresolved, with the PRC claiming the island as a province it has never administered.

quickFacts.label: Governing polity
quickFacts.value: The Republic of China (ROC), whose central government relocated to Taiwan in December 1949

quickFacts.label: PRC claim
quickFacts.value: The People's Republic of China claims Taiwan as a province; it has never governed the island

quickFacts.label: De jure status
quickFacts.value: Contested and internationally unresolved; no peace treaty has settled the cross-strait question

quickFacts.label: Earliest inhabitants
quickFacts.value: Austronesian-speaking peoples; Taiwan is widely regarded as the homeland of the Austronesian language family

quickFacts.label: Japanese colonial rule
quickFacts.value: 1895 to 1945, following the Treaty of Shimonoseki

quickFacts.label: First direct presidential election
quickFacts.value: 1996, won by Lee Teng-hui after the 1987 lifting of martial law

eras.title: Austronesian Island and the European Outposts
eras.standfirst: Long inhabited by Austronesian-speaking peoples, Taiwan entered the records of global trade in the seventeenth century as Dutch and Spanish companies planted fortified outposts on its coasts.
body: For millennia before any state on the Asian mainland governed it, Taiwan was inhabited by Austronesian-speaking peoples, and the island is widely regarded by linguists as the homeland from which the Austronesian language family spread across maritime Southeast Asia and the Pacific. These communities lived by farming, hunting and fishing in distinct groups across the lowlands and mountains, and their descendants survive today as Taiwan's recognised Indigenous peoples. Han Chinese settlement from the mainland's coastal provinces remained limited until the seventeenth century, when European maritime trading companies began to take an interest in the island they called Formosa.

In 1624 the Dutch East India Company established a fortified trading base, Fort Zeelandia, on an islet off the southwestern coast near present-day Tainan, making Taiwan a node in the company's commerce across East Asia. The Dutch encouraged Han Chinese migration to farm sugar and rice, taxed trade and attempted to convert and administer Indigenous communities. In 1626 the Spanish, based in the Philippines, responded by occupying the northern coast, building forts at Keelung and Tamsui, but their foothold was short-lived: a Dutch expedition expelled the Spanish from the north in 1642.

Dutch rule lasted only about four decades. It was ended by Zheng Chenggong, known to Europeans as Koxinga, a commander loyal to the fallen Ming dynasty who sought a secure base after the Manchu Qing conquest of the mainland. In 1661 he landed on Taiwan with a large army and besieged the Dutch garrison; the surrender of Fort Zeelandia in February 1662 ended Dutch control of the island and opened a new chapter under Chinese-style government.

events.title: Dutch found Fort Zeelandia
events.summary: The Dutch East India Company establishes a fortified trading base, Fort Zeelandia, on the southwestern coast near present-day Tainan, beginning roughly four decades of Dutch commercial rule over parts of the island.

events.title: Spanish settle the northern coast
events.summary: Spanish forces from the Philippines occupy northern Taiwan, building forts at Keelung and Tamsui in response to the Dutch presence in the south.

events.title: Dutch expel the Spanish
events.summary: A Dutch expedition drives the Spanish from their northern outposts, leaving the Dutch East India Company as the dominant European power on the island.

events.title: Koxinga expels the Dutch
events.summary: After a siege begun in 1661, the Ming-loyalist commander Zheng Chenggong (Koxinga) forces the surrender of Fort Zeelandia, ending Dutch rule and founding the Zheng (Tungning) regime on Taiwan.

figures.name: Zheng Chenggong (Koxinga)
figures.role: Ming-loyalist commander; founder of the Zheng regime on Taiwan
figures.blurb: A loyal supporter of the last Ming claimant after the Manchu Qing conquest, he drove the Dutch East India Company from Taiwan in 1662 and established a Chinese-style government there as a base for the anti-Qing struggle.

pullquote.text: He turned his attention to Taiwan, from which he drove out a Dutch occupying force and established a government of his own.

eras.title: The Zheng Regime and Qing Rule
eras.standfirst: Koxinga's heirs ran an independent Ming-loyalist kingdom from Tainan until 1683, when the Qing dynasty absorbed Taiwan and administered it for more than two centuries, eventually raising it to a province.
body: Zheng Chenggong died in 1662, only months after his victory, but his family carried on the regime he had founded, centred on Tainan and often called the Kingdom of Tungning. Under his son Zheng Jing the Zheng administration governed the island, promoted Han Chinese settlement and agriculture, and sustained resistance to the Qing across the strait for two further decades. The kingdom was the first state on Taiwan modelled on Chinese institutions, with a Ming-style court and Confucian administration, even as it remained a redoubt of opposition to the new dynasty ruling the mainland.

The Qing dynasty finally moved to eliminate this base of Ming loyalism. In 1683 a Qing fleet defeated the Zheng forces, and the dynasty annexed Taiwan the following year, attaching it as a prefecture of Fujian province. For most of the next two centuries Qing administration concentrated on the western plains, where Han Chinese migration from Fujian and Guangdong steadily expanded farmland and towns, while large parts of the mountainous interior remained beyond effective government control and home to Indigenous communities.

Growing strategic interest in the island, sharpened by foreign pressure on China's coast, led the Qing to upgrade Taiwan's status. In 1885 the imperial government designated Taiwan a separate province, and the reforming first governor, Liu Mingchuan, promoted railways, telegraphs and other modernising projects. This period of intensified Qing investment proved brief: within a decade the island would be ceded to a rising imperial Japan.

events.title: Qing conquest of the Zheng regime
events.summary: A Qing fleet defeats the Zheng (Tungning) forces, ending the Ming-loyalist kingdom; Taiwan is annexed the following year and attached to Fujian province as a prefecture.

events.title: Taiwan made a Qing province
events.summary: The Qing government raises Taiwan to the status of a separate province, with Liu Mingchuan as reforming first governor promoting railways and modernisation.

eras.title: Fifty Years of Japanese Colonial Rule
eras.standfirst: Ceded to Japan after the First Sino-Japanese War, Taiwan spent half a century as a Japanese colony that combined infrastructure-building and economic development with armed pacification and assimilation policies.
body: Japan's victory in the First Sino-Japanese War of 1894 to 1895 brought Taiwan under Japanese sovereignty. By the Treaty of Shimonoseki, signed on 17 April 1895, the Qing empire ceded Taiwan and the Pescadores (Penghu) Islands to Japan along with other concessions. The cession was resisted on the island, where a short-lived republic was proclaimed in an attempt to forestall the handover, but Japanese forces suppressed armed opposition and consolidated control over the new colony.

Over the following fifty years Japan administered Taiwan as its first major overseas colony and invested heavily in it. Colonial authorities built railways, roads, ports and a public-health system, expanded sugar and rice production, and established schools, developing Taiwan as a productive and strategically valuable possession. This development was carried out under a centralised colonial government that relied on policing and surveillance and that suppressed both Han Chinese and Indigenous resistance, sometimes with considerable violence.

From the late 1930s, as Japan mobilised for war in China and the Pacific, colonial policy intensified assimilation through campaigns that promoted the Japanese language, names and customs and sought to remould Taiwanese identity. Taiwanese were conscripted and recruited into Japan's war effort. Japanese rule ended only with Japan's defeat in the Second World War in 1945, after which the island's administration was transferred to the Republic of China.

events.title: Treaty of Shimonoseki cedes Taiwan to Japan
events.summary: Ending the First Sino-Japanese War, the treaty obliges the Qing empire to cede Taiwan and the Pescadores to Japan, beginning fifty years of Japanese colonial rule.

events.title: Japanese colonial administration
events.summary: Japan develops Taiwan with railways, ports, public health and expanded agriculture while suppressing resistance and, from the late 1930s, pursuing intensive assimilation campaigns.

pullquote.text: China was obliged... to cede Taiwan, the Pescadores Islands, and the Liaodong (south Manchurian) Peninsula to Japan.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the Treaty of Shimonoseki

eras.title: Handover, the February 28 Incident, and the ROC Relocation
eras.standfirst: Republic of China forces took over Taiwan in 1945; harsh early administration sparked the February 28 Incident of 1947, and after defeat in the Chinese Civil War the ROC central government relocated to the island in December 1949.
body: Following Japan's surrender in 1945, Taiwan's administration was transferred to the Republic of China, then governed by Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalists (Kuomintang), and Nationalist authorities took over the island in October 1945. Early Nationalist rule quickly bred discontent: mismanagement, inflation, corruption and the heavy-handed conduct of the new administration alienated much of the local population, which had lived for half a century under a different state.

Tensions erupted on 28 February 1947 in the event known as the February 28 Incident, after agents enforcing a state cigarette monopoly assaulted a vendor in Taipei and then fired into an angry crowd. Protests spread across the island and were violently suppressed when Chiang sent troops, who killed thousands, many of them unarmed civilians, and targeted Taiwanese elites. The incident inaugurated decades of authoritarian repression of dissent later known as the White Terror, conducted under martial law.

Meanwhile the Nationalists were losing the resumed Chinese Civil War on the mainland to Mao Zedong's Communists, who proclaimed the People's Republic of China on 1 October 1949. Defeated, Chiang Kai-shek and his government withdrew to Taiwan, and the ROC central government relocated to Taipei in December 1949. This relocation established the present self-governing polity on the island, while the new PRC claimed Taiwan as part of its territory without ever administering it.

events.title: ROC takes over Taiwan
events.summary: After Japan's surrender, the Republic of China under the Nationalists (Kuomintang) takes over the administration of Taiwan from Japan.

events.title: February 28 Incident
events.summary: Protests against Nationalist rule, triggered by a violent monopoly-enforcement episode in Taipei, are crushed by troops; thousands are killed, beginning the long repression known as the White Terror.

events.title: People's Republic of China proclaimed
events.summary: Having defeated the Nationalists in the Chinese Civil War, Mao Zedong proclaims the People's Republic of China on the mainland; Chiang and his forces withdraw toward Taiwan.

events.title: ROC government relocates to Taiwan
events.summary: Chiang Kai-shek's Republic of China central government relocates to Taipei after defeat in the civil war, founding the present self-governing polity on the island; this was not a declaration of independence.

figures.name: Chiang Kai-shek
figures.role: Leader of the Nationalists (Kuomintang); head of the ROC government on Taiwan
figures.blurb: Head of the Nationalist government in China from 1928 to 1949, he led the Kuomintang to defeat in the Chinese Civil War and relocated the Republic of China government to Taiwan in 1949, ruling the island until his death in 1975.

eras.title: Cold War, the 'Two Chinas' Question, and the Economic Miracle
eras.standfirst: Shielded by the United States, the ROC held China's UN seat until 1971 and Washington's recognition until 1979; meanwhile, under one-party martial law, Taiwan achieved a celebrated export-led economic miracle.
body: For its first decades on Taiwan the ROC presented itself as the legitimate government of all China, and during the early Cold War it was recognised as such by the United States and held China's seat at the United Nations, including a permanent place on the Security Council. The PRC, governing the mainland, maintained that there was only one China and that Taiwan was part of it. This 'two Chinas' contest was decided in international forums in the 1970s: in October 1971 the UN General Assembly seated the PRC's representatives in China's place, and in the Shanghai Communiqué of 1972 the United States began moving toward Beijing.

The diplomatic shift culminated in 1979, when the United States established formal relations with the PRC, recognised it as the sole legal government of China, and ended official recognition of the ROC; the U.S. Congress simultaneously passed the Taiwan Relations Act to continue unofficial ties and provide for the island's defence. Taiwan thereby lost most formal diplomatic recognition even as it remained self-governing, and its international legal status stayed contested and unresolved between the competing claims of the two governments.

Domestically these were decades of one-party Nationalist rule under martial law, but also of extraordinary economic transformation. Through land reform and an export-led industrial strategy, Taiwan grew rapidly from a largely agricultural society into a manufacturing and trading power, becoming one of the 'Four Asian Tigers'. Among the cultural legacies of the era was the National Palace Museum in Taipei, opened in 1965 to house a vast collection of Chinese imperial art and antiquities, many crates of which had been moved from the mainland around 1948 to 1949.

events.title: UN seat transferred to the PRC
events.summary: The UN General Assembly votes to seat the People's Republic of China in China's place at the United Nations; the ROC's representatives lose the seat. The resolution does not address Taiwan's sovereignty.

events.title: Shanghai Communiqué
events.summary: During President Nixon's visit to China, the United States and the PRC issue the Shanghai Communiqué; the U.S. acknowledges the position that there is but one China, beginning normalisation.

events.title: U.S. switches recognition to the PRC
events.summary: The United States establishes formal diplomatic relations with the PRC as the sole legal government of China and ends recognition of the ROC; Congress passes the Taiwan Relations Act to sustain unofficial ties.

events.title: National Palace Museum opens in Taipei
events.summary: A permanent home opens in Taipei for a vast collection of Chinese imperial art and antiquities, much of it relocated from the mainland around 1948 to 1949.

pullquote.text: The United States... recognized the PRC as 'the sole legal government of China' and withdrawing recognition from Taiwan.
pullquote.attribution: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian, on the 1979 normalization

eras.title: Democratisation and the Cross-Strait Dispute
eras.standfirst: The lifting of martial law in 1987 began a transition to multiparty democracy, capped by the first direct presidential election in 1996; the cross-strait dispute over Taiwan's status remains unresolved into 2026.
body: Taiwan's political transformation began under President Lee Teng-hui, who succeeded Chiang Ching-kuo and ended martial law in 1987 along with the ban on travel to the mainland. Over the following years he advanced constitutional reform and opened the political system, broadening representation and legalising opposition parties. The process reached a milestone in 1996, when Lee won Taiwan's first direct presidential election, becoming the island's first popularly elected president and completing its transition into a competitive multiparty democracy.

Since democratisation, governing power on Taiwan has alternated between the Nationalists (Kuomintang) and the Democratic Progressive Party through regular elections, and the island has remained a prosperous, high-technology economy. Throughout, the fundamental dispute over its status has persisted. The ROC continues to govern Taiwan, the Penghu (Pescadores), and other islands; the PRC claims Taiwan as a province and states that it seeks unification, by force if necessary, while never having administered the island.

As of 2026 this cross-strait question remains internationally unresolved. No peace treaty has ever formally ended the state of conflict between the two governments, most states maintain official relations with the PRC rather than the ROC, and the parties continue to advance opposing positions on Taiwan's status. The factual situation is that the ROC governs the island and the PRC claims but does not control it; the de jure status is contested, and this entry describes the positions of the parties without endorsing any of them.

events.title: Martial law lifted
events.summary: President Lee Teng-hui ends nearly four decades of martial law and the ban on travel to the mainland, opening the way to democratic reform.

events.title: First direct presidential election
events.summary: Lee Teng-hui wins Taiwan's first direct, popular presidential election, becoming its first democratically elected president and completing the democratic transition.

events.title: Ongoing cross-strait dispute
events.summary: Into 2026 the dispute remains unresolved: the ROC governs Taiwan while the PRC claims it as a province without controlling it, and no peace treaty has settled the question.

figures.name: Lee Teng-hui
figures.role: President of the Republic of China (1988-2000)
figures.blurb: Succeeding to the presidency in 1988, he ended martial law, drove constitutional reform, and in 1996 won Taiwan's first direct presidential election, presiding over the island's transition to democracy.

pullquote.text: In 1996 he won the island's first free and direct presidential elections and became Taiwan's first democratically elected president.

figures.name: Zheng Chenggong (Koxinga)
figures.role: Ming-loyalist commander; founder of the Zheng regime on Taiwan
figures.blurb: He expelled the Dutch East India Company from Taiwan in 1662 and founded a Chinese-style government on the island as a base of resistance to the Qing dynasty; his heirs ruled until the Qing conquest of 1683.

figures.name: Chiang Kai-shek
figures.role: Leader of the Nationalists; head of the ROC government on Taiwan
figures.blurb: After leading the Nationalists to defeat in the Chinese Civil War, he relocated the Republic of China government to Taiwan in 1949 and ruled the island under martial law until his death in 1975.

figures.name: Lee Teng-hui
figures.role: President of the Republic of China (1988-2000)
figures.blurb: He ended martial law in 1987 and led Taiwan's democratisation, winning the island's first direct presidential election in 1996 as its first democratically elected president.

sources.label: Rapprochement with China, 1972
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian
